overall_summary: Paces AI Inc. argues that manual interconnection study processes and speculative, project-by-project load signals are the core operational problem, and that AI agent automation can cut study times from weeks to hours while land-aware planning maps direct upgrades to where load will actually be built. The company asks FERC to endorse AI automation with engineer sign-off, set a sub-20-business-day study SLA, require parcel-level planning maps, and tie expedited interconnection to locations that are power-ready and dispatchable.

quotes.text: interconnection studies up to 20x faster with full audit trails and sign-off
quotes.concern: AI agents can dramatically speed up interconnection studies while preserving auditability

quotes.text: manual study processes and speculative, project-by-project signals are producing slow interconnection queues and plans for load that will never be built, while a small set of constraints strands the load that actually can be built
quotes.concern: manual and speculative processes mismatch grid plans with real load locations

quotes.text: FERC's large-loads rule is the chance to fix both problems
quotes.concern: filer frames the rulemaking as the right vehicle to address study speed and planning mismatch

quotes.text: State that transmission providers are encouraged to use AI agents to operate approved study tools and scripts, as long as qualified engineers own the methodology and sign-off and workflows are auditable.
quotes.concern: FERC should explicitly endorse AI automation within existing reliability frameworks

quotes.text: Encourage or require a SLA of <20 business days for all large load initial studies which makes the leveraging of AI automation essential to meet such a timeline.
quotes.concern: a short study SLA would drive adoption of AI tools

quotes.text: Direct transmission providers that face material large-load growth to build parcel-level maps of developable large-load sites (zoning, setbacks, floodplains, water, ownership, etc.) and overlay them with bus-level withdrawal headroom.
quotes.concern: land-aware planning maps should be a required input to transmission planning

quotes.text: sited at buses with positive headroom and nearby developable land
quotes.concern: expedited treatment should be conditioned on dispatchability and power-ready siting

quotes.text: Permit slower timelines and clearer upgrade expectations for heavily constrained, non-power-ready locations.
quotes.concern: constrained locations should face longer timelines and explicit upgrade cost expectations

quotes.text: the real operational risk is mis-located, speculative load plus manual-only study processes that cannot scale
quotes.concern: speculative and mislocated load combined with manual studies is the central reliability risk

bins.name: Study SLA and fast track
bins.description: Paces supports expedited treatment and recommends a mandatory SLA of under 20 business days for initial studies. It argues that AI automation is the only way to meet such a timeline at scale, and that the fastest interconnection track should be reserved for dispatchable loads sited at power-ready buses.

bins.name: Upgrade expectations at constrained sites
bins.description: Paces supports giving constrained, non-power-ready locations slower timelines paired with clearer upgrade cost expectations, so that speculative projects at costly sites face transparent signals rather than joining the same queue as well-sited loads.

bins.name: AI-automated interconnection studies
bins.description: Paces argues that manual study processes cannot scale to meet large-load demand and endorses AI agents running approved power-flow tools under engineer oversight. It recommends FERC explicitly permit this approach and pair it with a short study SLA to make automation necessary.

bins.name: Dispatchability as fast-track condition
bins.description: Paces supports tying the fastest interconnection track to loads that are dispatchable or curtailable, treating flexibility as a prerequisite for expedited treatment rather than an optional feature.

bins.name: Land-aware, constraint-aware siting
bins.description: Paces argues that speculative, poorly sited load produces grid plans that do not match where load will actually be built. It recommends requiring parcel-level developability maps overlaid with bus-level headroom so planning and interconnection decisions reflect actual siting constraints.

bins.name: FERC endorsement of AI agents
bins.description: Paces asks FERC to formally endorse AI agent automation inside existing reliability frameworks, provided engineers retain sign-off authority and workflows produce auditable outputs. It also requests a technical conference where it can demonstrate real-world examples.

bins.name: Parcel-level planning maps
bins.description: Paces recommends requiring transmission providers facing material large-load growth to build parcel-level maps covering zoning, setbacks, floodplains, water access, and ownership, then overlay those maps with bus-level withdrawal headroom to guide both planning and siting decisions.
